title: Born the Year the War Began
summary: Interviewed by David Cline in San Diego for the Civil Rights History Project, with Guha Shankar of the Library of Congress and cameraman John Bishop, Mateo Camarillo opens by placing his birth in Tijuana in 1941, his US-citizen mother Rufina who built B-29 bombers at Convair and worked the tuna factory in Barrio Logan, his father from Nuevo Leon, and the family's 1950 immigration to San Diego with his brother and two sisters.

title: What Did She Say?
topic: A Spanish Speaker in Second Grade
summary: Camarillo describes a mother from Campo whose two brothers died serving in World War II, his own later sense of obligation to serve, and arriving in the second grade with no English and no bilingual education, failing the English test, and huddling with other Spanish-speaking children in a two-grade farm-labor schoolroom asking each other what the teacher had said.

title: We Were Human Beings Just Like They Were
summary: He recalls first thinking he and his classmates were mentally retarded because they could not communicate, learning English by trial and error on the playground where Anglo students wanted them on their teams, his mother's job in agricultural Mission Valley near the Junipero Serra mission, and starting work at age ten in an immigrant community that served as a support system.

title: Others Defined Us and We Accepted It
summary: Camarillo traces how the family was identified as Mexican, then gradually called themselves Mexican American as they strove to participate, and how Chicano, at first a slur, was later adopted as a proud term for those struggling for equal rights, noting that schools taught nothing about Mexico so he stayed connected by visiting Tijuana.

title: The Crossing Guard Captain
summary: He describes building confidence and leadership through sports, heading the junior crossing-patrol guards, running cross country and track from junior high through Herbert Hoover High School, winning two scholarships including one from Allied Gardens, and becoming the first in his family to attend college while holding three jobs.

title: Viva Kennedy
summary: Camarillo recounts graduating from San Diego State in four years, President Kennedy delivering the 1962 commencement address, his involvement in the Viva Kennedy Clubs, becoming a naturalized citizen at twenty-one through his mother, and launching the first multicultural voter registration project in San Diego with the Southwest Voter Registration Education Project, reaching African Americans, Asian Americans, and the county's fourteen Native American tribes.

title: You're Not Even Going to Be Served
topic: First Taste of the Deep South
summary: Camarillo tells of volunteering for the Army in 1965 out of gratitude rather than fleeing to Canada or Mexico like some friends, being sent to Georgia because of spinal meningitis in California, and a Charleston, South Carolina restaurant where he, an African American, and a Puerto Rican soldier waited hours only to be told they would not be served.

title: We Were All on the Same Side
summary: He recounts being refused entry to Atlanta nightclubs for lack of a membership card, contrasting that blatant Southern discrimination with California, and describing the Army as very integrated, the track team they called the road rats, and a basic-training ethic of taking orders where Anglo officers led a mostly minority enlisted ranks.

title: Scoutmaster of Europe
summary: Camarillo explains being spared Vietnam when the Army shifted toward experienced soldiers, sent instead to Germany as backfill in the Fourth Armored battalion, finishing a week of training-officer work in a single day, and being named Scoutmaster of the Transatlantic Council, traveling across Europe with the dependent children of soldiers.

title: Sixty Cases, or Six Hundred
summary: Returning to San Diego, he joins the welfare department as one of very few Spanish-speaking social workers, where English-speaking colleagues carried caseloads of sixty families while he carried six hundred, prompting him to organize across probation, the sheriff, and other departments and to found Trabajadores de la Raza to recruit bilingual workers.

title: A National Network of Workers
summary: Camarillo describes Trabajadores de la Raza becoming a national organization with chapters from Los Angeles and San Jose to San Antonio, Houston, and the East Coast, the San Diego chapter serving all eighteen cities of the county, the work done after hours and funded at first through membership dues.

title: A Bonus for Two Languages
summary: He recounts persuading the county board of supervisors to pay a ten percent bonus to Spanish-speaking workers doing twice the work, then winning the same from the city and from San Diego Gas and Electric, and working with legislators Richard Alatorre and Mervyn Dymally to establish a statewide bilingual pay program in the late 1970s.

title: Bilingual Education Called Un-American
topic: The Grand Jury and a Food Program
summary: Camarillo names equal treatment as the era's central issue, telling how the San Diego County Grand Jury called bilingual education un-American until a fifteen-organization ad hoc committee demanded the jurors be replaced, and how, through Trabajadores de la Raza, the coalition won the county its first food program, San Diego being the only one of California's fifty-eight counties without one, starting in San Ysidro on the border with the San Ysidro Women's Club.

title: They Risked Losing Their Jobs
summary: As executive director of the Chicano Federation, an umbrella for Spanish-speaking organizations, Camarillo recounts county employees streaming into his office over job discrimination, gathering six Latino attorneys in 1974, and warning the workers that litigation could cost them their jobs, a risk they accepted in pursuit of justice.

title: Sixty-Seven Percent Turned Down
summary: He describes suing San Diego County under newly passed equal employment opportunity law in what he calls the longest-lasting civil rights case in US history, joined by African American, Asian American, and Filipino plaintiffs, overturning a system in which sixty-seven percent of Hispanic applicants were rejected, and reflecting that no single group could have removed the barriers alone because all the county's minority communities shared the same lack of opportunity.

title: Not the Intent of Congress
summary: Camarillo recounts how Nixon's General Revenue Sharing sent no-strings funds to local government, how the Chicano Federation with chairman Dr. Alfredo Velasco allied with the largely Anglo Community Congress of the hippie beach communities and traveled to Washington, and how they rallied social service agencies and United Way to sue when the city tried to redefine who was poor or senior, terms already fixed by the Older Americans Act, winning over five million dollars the first year and over six million the next.

title: Chapters Across the Country
summary: He describes staying in communication with Trabajadores de la Raza chapters in Los Angeles, San Jose, Houston, New York, and Denver, the Spanish-speaking organizations being the largest ethnic group, and joining the staff of the Chicano Federation, the first multi-discipline Spanish-speaking organization in the county, as executive director.

title: I Trained Under Saul Alinsky
topic: San Jose State and the School of Social Work
summary: Camarillo recounts being recruited by the strong San Jose chapter to help start a school of social work, using his GI Bill to earn a master's in community organizing at San Diego State while working for the county, and winning a Ford Foundation Fellowship to train under Saul Alinsky in Chicago, the organizer who he says trained Cesar Chavez and the founders of the National Council of La Raza.

title: Recruiting Herman Gallegos
summary: He describes leaving San Diego State to become coordinator of field instruction at San Jose, building the school of social work, and recruiting Herman Gallegos, a National Council of La Raza founder who grew up in Sal Si Puedes alongside Cesar Chavez and who knew how to access Ford and Rockefeller Foundation capital the state would not provide.

part: Chicano Park and the Politics of the Phone
title: They Took the Land With Picks and Shovels
summary: Camarillo explains that his Chicano Federation offices sat under the San Diego-Coronado Bridge, and recounts how, when the city reneged on a promised park and planned a highway patrol station on the state land, his sister Tomasa Camarillo led the community to physically occupy the site and build Chicano Park by hand.

title: The Birth of the Mural Movement
summary: He describes the artists among the Chicano Park steering committee deciding to beautify the raw cement bridge pillars, giving birth to the mural movement that depicts the history of the Chicano movement, and the federally funded facility built there instead of a highway patrol station that housed the Brown Berets, the Pinto Union of ex-convicts, state employment counseling, and welfare social workers in a humane, multicultural demonstration project.

title: We Exchanged Phone Numbers
topic: An Alliance With the Police Chief
summary: He describes testifying for new police chief Bill Kolender, who as a Jewish man understood discrimination, the two agreeing to meet regularly and trade phone numbers so a single call could stop police in hot pursuit from running through his building full of seniors and women, and a similar standing arrangement with Mayor Pete Wilson.

title: You Better Get Your Ass Down Here
summary: Camarillo tells how two officers off shift at 2 a.m. donned gloves and beat members of the Brown Image low-rider car club and their girlfriends in Barrio Logan, how he summoned Mayor Pete Wilson, Chief Kolender, and the city manager to a 5 p.m. meeting with five hundred low riders where the police apologized, and how a single call to Kolender also ended police directing taxi drivers to take Mexican-looking residents to immigration, the kind of communication that spared San Diego the riots of Watts and Detroit.

title: Stupid They Are Not
summary: Camarillo argues that officials paid attention because Hispanics were the largest minority and held the power of the vote, that public officials prefer peaceful resolution to violence in which they too might be hurt, and that the movement worked through litigation, negotiation, and mediation first, joining Cesar Chavez's nonviolent boycott marches when needed.

title: When the Federation Spoke
summary: He describes drawing a salary leading a federation whose board were heads of other organizations, so it spoke for all the county's Spanish-speaking groups, and using a political action committee and self-formed advisory commissions to influence mayors, supervisors, and assembly members at a time when San Diego rarely had even two Latino council members.

title: Don't Bite the Hand That Feeds You
summary: Camarillo explains that suing the city, county, and state while taking their grants was unsustainable, so in 1976 he chose the private sector for an independent income, researching the food industry and settling on franchising as a proven system to learn business.

title: Why Am I Not Qualified?
summary: Turned down twice as unqualified despite knowing Jack in the Box founder Robert Silverman, Camarillo recounts inviting the elected officials he knew, the congressman, councilman, supervisors, and assemblyman, to his third meeting with regional manager Dean, since restaurants need over a dozen government permits, asking Dean to explain to them why he was not qualified, winning an immediate yes, and going on to build half a dozen radio stations and over a dozen other businesses while giving free french fries for March Fong Eu's voter registration drive.

title: A Voice for Spanish Speakers
summary: Camarillo explains buying radio stations because information is freedom, San Diego having no Spanish-language stations and only distant signals from Tijuana and Los Angeles indifferent to local news, and recounts applying in 1980 after Reagan's deregulation freed a 1040 AM frequency on the West Coast, competing against applicants statewide and prevailing with his multicultural company Quetzal Bilingual Communications, named for the Aztec bird.

title: How Many Meals Can You Eat?
summary: He describes leveraging the win to build satellite-connected stations up the California coast, the Port Commission calling him to broadcast announcements when junkyards, Monsanto, and the tuna factory released toxic gas over Barrio Logan, and reflects that he has dedicated his life to public service even in the private sector, giving away product and registering voters without pay because he believes America is about helping your fellow man.

title: A Little Sugar in the Conversation
summary: Camarillo explains pressuring officials with common sense and softening it with money through political action committees, organizing MacPack among McDonald's franchisees and the Hispanic Golden 500 whose members were taxed five hundred dollars, and contributing to Jerry Brown, Gray Davis, and Cruz Bustamante.

title: Six Judges at Once
summary: He recounts that when Jerry Brown, sympathetic and dating Linda Ronstadt, had six judicial openings in San Diego, Camarillo nominated all six Hispanic lawyers and all were appointed, that he was placed on a pension-fund board, and that even Pete Wilson took his contributions despite a hostility to Hispanics whose anti-immigrant resolution he argues cost Republicans every statewide race for over twenty years except Arnold Schwarzenegger's.

part: The Sign of an Educated Person
title: A Horrific Graduation Rate
summary: He observes that public schools have changed but not enough, over half of entering students being Hispanic yet the graduation rate horrific, defending bilingual education as a transitional starting point and the California push for multicultural curriculum so students learn about their own cultures in a Southwest that was Mexican for centuries.

title: La Raza Cosmica
summary: Camarillo recounts a college teacher expert in the Aztecs, Mayans, and Toltecs inspiring him to name companies Quetzal, Quinto Sol, and Aztlan, arguing that knowing other cultures is the sign of an educated person, and tracing the term La Raza to Jose Vasconcelos's post-Revolution book La Raza Cosmica.

title: Pardon for Those Already Here
summary: He describes joining the National Council of La Raza, serving the maximum terms, working with President Reagan and Senators Orrin Hatch and Edward Kennedy on the immigration reform Reagan signed, which he frames as legalizing people already here and employed, and serving over twenty years on the Greenlining Institute board, named as the opposite of redlining, chairing the state's telephone deregulation and delivering services in ten languages.
related_events: Immigration Reform and Control Act of 1986

title: Hot Lunches Over Education
summary: Camarillo describes his first run for office, for the district-wide school board because education is key, against a senior citizen backed by an assemblyman's resources, and his post-election analysis finding only nineteen percent of voters had children in school while over sixty percent were seniors interested in hot lunches.

title: A Brother to Defend Him
summary: He recounts running next for the Southwestern Community College district adjacent to Tijuana, where most underserved students go after high school, securing a unanimous promise of support from Latino leaders, only to be undercut when a professor in trouble had his brother run, splitting the Hispanic vote against a wealthy Coronado candidate.

title: One Person, One Vote
topic: Redrawing the Lines and a Last Run
summary: Camarillo describes thirty years of redistricting beginning in 1980, redrawing state assembly and senate lines, then county lines, then being selected by judges to redistrict the city of San Diego, and chairing the Latino redistricting committee after the 2010 census to create the first majority-Latino Ninth District, where he was asked to run for city council but fewer than ten percent of voters turned out and a well-known newscaster incumbent won, his last campaign.

title: Breathalyzers and a German Maker
summary: Camarillo describes the for-profit business with a social benefit he runs with his wife, installing breathalyzer devices in the cars of people cited for driving under the influence and representing a German manufacturer that has made gas-activation devices since World War I, and recounts his wife pushing him to write his autobiography Journey in Search of the American Dream, reaffirming that everything meaningful he has done was done with others.

title: A Country of Immigrants
summary: Camarillo argues the United States is a country of immigrants going back before the Pilgrims, symbolized by the French gift of the Statue of Liberty, and that immigrants take jobs others reject, recalling strawberries rotting in Oregon's Willamette Valley for want of workers and schools closing early so children could pick them.

title: Walls Are Not the Answer
summary: He notes research showing immigrants contribute billions and high-tech innovation, yet face the charge of taking jobs and newer fears since the Middle East wars of hostile arrivals, and references a presidential candidate talking of building walls and deporting, concluding that a regulated process makes sense but walls do not.

title: Get It Into the Hands of Those Who Need It
summary: Camarillo closes by saying he is honored to share experiences of contributions long ignored, expressing his wish to learn how the project's end product will be distributed and made accessible, since education opens minds to the full society, before the recording ends with the standard Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.
